questions.scenario:
You want to add a step that any Jenkinsfile can call as `buildJavaService(...)`. The step renders an entire declarative pipeline.

Where does it go?

options.text: `vars/buildJavaService.groovy` with a `def call(Map config = [:]) { pipeline { ... } }` entry point.
`src/com/example/BuildJavaService.groovy` as a class.
`resources/build-java-service.yaml`.
Inlined into every Jenkinsfile that needs it.

questions.explanation: Global steps callable as a top-level identifier from a Jenkinsfile live in `vars/`. The filename is the step name. The `def call(...)` form is what makes `buildJavaService(...)` work as a method call. `src/` is for helper classes that you import; you cannot directly call a `src/` class as a top-level step. `resources/` is for non-code template files.

questions.scenario:
You want to extract a `SemverHelper` class that parses and compares version strings, then call it from several `vars/` steps.

Where does the class go?

options.text: `vars/SemverHelper.groovy`.
`src/com/devpolaris/SemverHelper.groovy` with a package declaration; import from the global step via `import com.devpolaris.SemverHelper`.
`resources/SemverHelper.txt`.
Helpers cannot be reused across `vars/` steps.

questions.explanation: `src/` is the standard Java/Groovy source tree for the shared library: real classes with packages and imports. Global steps in `vars/` then `import com.devpolaris.SemverHelper` like any other class. Putting the class under `vars/` would expose it as a top-level step (callable as `SemverHelper(...)` from a Jenkinsfile), which is rarely what you want.

questions.scenario:
You want to ship a default `kustomization.yaml` template that pipeline steps can render with build-time substitutions, instead of inlining the YAML in Groovy strings.

Where does the template go?

options.text: `resources/templates/kustomization.yaml`. Read it from `vars/` with `libraryResource('templates/kustomization.yaml')`.
`vars/kustomization.yaml`.
`src/templates/kustomization.yaml`.
Templates must be inlined as Groovy strings.

questions.explanation: `resources/` is exactly for non-code files (templates, default configs, generated test data). The `libraryResource(path)` step reads them at runtime. Putting non-code under `vars/` or `src/` does not work because those trees are compiled. Inlining as Groovy strings is fine for short snippets but unwieldy for full configs.

questions.scenario: Which of these are real reasons to keep most logic in `src/` classes rather than directly in `vars/` global steps?

options.text: Classes can be unit-tested with JenkinsPipelineUnit or similar; `vars/` steps are harder to mock.
Classes can have private methods; `vars/` exposes everything as top-level callable.
Classes can extend each other and use polymorphism; `vars/` steps cannot.
`src/` classes load faster than `vars/` steps.

questions.explanation: Testability, encapsulation, and composability are the load-bearing reasons. Load time is not a meaningful difference. The pattern: thin `vars/` steps that delegate to classes in `src/`, with templates in `resources/`. This is also how the article's `devpolaris-pipeline` library is structured.

questions.scenario:
A junior engineer puts a 600-line `def call(Map config = [:]) { ... }` in `vars/buildJavaService.groovy` with all the helper functions defined as nested closures inside `call`.

What is the right refactor recommendation?

options.text: Extract helpers to `src/com/devpolaris/Build.groovy` (or similar) as proper class methods, leave `vars/buildJavaService.groovy` as a thin wrapper that constructs the helper and orchestrates.
Leave it; nested closures are idiomatic.
Move everything to `resources/` as a template.
Convert the file to Java.

questions.explanation: Nested closures inside `call` are the classic shape of a `vars/` step that has outgrown itself. The refactor is to pull helpers into a `src/` class with real methods (testable, reusable, namespaced), then keep the global step as the thin entry point. Templates under `resources/` are for non-code. Java would lose Groovy's pipeline-step DSL.
